questions: A long-running online television episode guide, active since the mid-1990s, provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages but links each episode to external databases for expanded information. Identify the two third-party television databases it directs users to for detailed episode data. For each database, describe the types of additional information users can typically find there (such as content beyond titles and airdates), and explain how the site instructs users to submit corrections or updates to individual episodes.

Identify the long-running online television reference project founded in 1995 that specializes in listing episode titles and original airdates for more than 12,000 TV series. Without naming the site in your reasoning process, describe its core cataloging approach, how it connects users to expanded episode information, the special "printable" feature it offers for complete episode summaries, and the types of schedule grids and regional listings it maintains. Additionally, explain at least three other resources or sections the site provides beyond standard episode lists (such as data downloads, related media links, apps, merchandise, radio listings, or FAQs), and summarize how users can submit corrections or updates. Your answer should synthesize information from multiple sections of the platform’s publicly available materials.

Identify the long-running online television reference guide that has been cataloging TV episode information since 1995 and now tracks over 12,000 series. Based on publicly available information about this site, describe its core purpose and episode-listing format, specify which external database its episode titles commonly link to for expanded details, explain how a user can access a consolidated summary of all episodes for a show, and state what type of viewing-related links appear at the bottom of each show’s page. Additionally, list at least four different regional schedule grids or country-specific sections it provides, and mention two other TV-related resources or features highlighted on its main menu (such as downloadable data formats, themed media, apps, merchandise links, or related web directories).

rounds.question: A long-running online television episode guide, active since the mid-1990s, provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages but links each episode to external databases for expanded information. Identify the two third-party television databases it directs users to for detailed episode data. For each database, describe the types of additional information users can typically find there (such as content beyond titles and airdates), and explain how the site instructs users to submit corrections or updates to individual episodes.
checklist_width: TVmaze identified as one of the linked databases
Description that TVmaze provides additional details such as guest stars and plot summaries
TV.com identified as the other database referenced for corrections/updates
Explanation that corrections and updates to individual episodes should be sent to the editors at TVmaze or TV.com via those sites
checklist_depth: TVmaze + Correctly identified as an external episode database linked for expanded episode information (guest stars, plot summaries)
TV.com + Correctly identified as an external site where users can send corrections and updates to episode information
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the functionality and history of the episode guide site without naming it, requiring identification through its distinctive features (titles/airdates only, mid-1990s origin, external linking model). It then requires wide aggregation by asking for details about both third-party databases—what information they provide and how corrections are handled—ensuring the respondent must gather and synthesize information about multiple entities rather than relying on a single fact.
rounds.answer_a:
The long-running episode guide is **epguides.com**, established in 1995. It directs users to **The Internet Movie Database (IMDb)** and **TV.com** for expanded details.

On IMDb, users can access comprehensive information including full cast and crew credits, detailed episode synopses, trivia, goofs, quotes, and user reviews. TV.com typically offers episode summaries, recaps, guest star details, discussion forums, and sometimes multimedia content like photos or video clips [1].

To submit corrections or updates regarding episode titles and airdates to the guide itself, epguides instructs users to send an email to the site's webmaster. The site explicitly requests that users do not use the correction forms of the linked databases (like IMDb or TV.com) to fix errors on the epguides pages, as the data must be updated at the source [2].

## References

[1] epguides.com - "About" page, detailing the purpose of the site and external links.
[2] epguides.com - "FAQ" or "Contact" page, outlining the email submission process for data corrections.

rounds.answer_a:
The website founded in 1995 that catalogs over 12,000 TV series episodes is Epguides.com, a comprehensive television reference project. The site's core cataloging approach organizes shows alphabetically with episode numbers, titles, original air dates, and brief plot summaries [1]. For expanded information, each episode connects users to detailed external sources like IMDb links, providing comprehensive cast, crew, and production details [2].

The site's distinctive "printable" feature generates complete episode summaries without extraneous website elements, allowing users to create clean reference lists for personal use [3]. Epguides maintains extensive schedule grids showing current and upcoming programming across multiple channels, along with regional listings for different time zones and countries [4].

Beyond standard episode lists, the platform offers downloadable data files for developers, links to related media including official show websites, mobile applications for on-the-go access, and merchandise featuring TV-related content [5]. Users can submit corrections or updates through the site's contact form, with contributions reviewed by volunteer editors who maintain accuracy and completeness of the database [6].

## References
[1] Epguides.com - "About Us" page
[2] Epguides.com - Site features and navigation
[3] Epguides.com - Printable episode summaries
[4] Epguides.com - Schedule grids and regional listings
[5] Epguides.com - Additional resources section
[6] Epguides.com - Submission guidelines and corrections policy

Based on publicly available information about this site, describe its core purpose and episode-listing format, specify which external database its episode titles commonly link to for expanded details, explain how a user can access a consolidated summary of all episodes for a show, and state what type of viewing-related links appear at the bottom of each show’s page. rounds.answer_a:
Epguides.com is the long-running online television reference guide established in 1995 that catalogs information for over 12,000 series [1]. Its core purpose is to provide a comprehensive, user-friendly database of episode listings, organized alphabetically by show title. Each episode entry typically displays the season, episode number, production code, title, and original air date [1].

For expanded details, the episode titles commonly link to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), allowing users to access cast, crew, and plot summaries [2]. To access a consolidated summary of all episodes for a specific show without extraneous web elements, users can utilize the distinctive "Print" button located at the top of each series page, which generates a clean text list suitable for offline reference [3].

At the bottom of each show’s page, viewing-related links direct users to external resources for purchasing DVDs, finding show merchandise, or accessing official network sites [4]. The site maintains extensive schedule grids, including specific sections for US Eastern, US Central, US Pacific, and Canadian listings [5]. Additionally, the main menu highlights other TV-related resources such as "Web Links," a directory of related television websites, and "Downloads," offering data files in CSV or plain text formats for offline use [6].

## References
[1] Epguides.com - Main Page / FAQ
[2] Epguides.com - General Site Features
[3] Epguides.com - "Printable" Guide Feature
[4] Epguides.com - Individual Series Page Structure
[5] Epguides.com - Schedule Grids & Regional Listings
[6] Epguides.com - Main Menu Resources

First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies epguides.com as the long-running TV episode guide founded in 1995 covering over 12,000 series. Agent B fails to identify any entity and refuses to answer, so it fails the Core Entity check (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers many required elements—founding date (1995), scope (12,000+ series), episode format (titles and airdates), printable summary option, regional schedule grids, and additional features like downloads and web links. However, it contains notable factual errors: it claims episode titles link to IMDb (ground truth: TVmaze), and misstates the type of viewing-related links (should be streaming video sources). Its regional examples are also weaker than the expected country-based sections. So A has WIDE inaccuracies, but it still addresses most checklist components.

Agent B provides no substantive answer and therefore fails all WIDTH requirements.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A delivers a structured, readable, citation-supported response that attempts to fully answer the query. Agent B provides a refusal, offering no practical value.

Because Agent A identifies the correct entity and attempts comprehensive coverage (despite factual mistakes), and Agent B fails the core task entirely, Agent A wins. However, due to A’s factual errors, the verdict is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER.
